author: @waltdeheer
text:
Hi, I wonder if you have ever heard of Aquadag. It is interesting stuff. It is conducting nanoscopic graphitic ink, discovered around 1900, by Acheson. It has been used extensively in the electronics industry ever since. It has recently been rediscovered and it is now called "graphene ink". 
Few know, that very many "graphene" products and discoveries are simply renamed graphite products and discoveries. Remember: graphene is a monolayer of graphite. Once you stack it (with more that 4 or so layers), it is graphite, and behaves exactly like graphite, whose properties (electronic, chemical, mechanical) have been known and used for a very, very long time. 
Indeed, graphite is a very important material. It doesn't get better simply by renaming it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - absolutely mate and i do know about aquadag - still - a new name will help you get a patent lol

author: @JazenValencia
text:
Here's something for you to ponder.  I saw this years ago and your video reminded me of it. Deborah Chung's Negative Resistor.  

Professor Deborah Chung { [30] } at University of Buffalo also has invented a true negative resistor utilizing crossed carbon filaments, and thoroughly tested it.  The university filed a patent application and moved toward licensing for commercial applications.  However, the university's web site abruptly pulled off the University's offer of a technical package to companies signing nondisclosure and wishing to license the technology. 

Chung's paper was submitted to a journal and after a protracted period in review was finally published. [9]   It appears that the university has classified defense contracts, and the Chung negative resistor may have been classified or made totally proprietary for use on such programs.

However, J.-L. Naudin in France has replicated the Chung-type negative resistance experiment, as well as several versions which can be performed by experimenters much more readily. [10]   The reader is referred to his website.10
[30] .        For a skeptical reference, see K. C. Cole, "Experts Scoff at Claim of Electricity Flowing with 'Negative Resistance'," Los Angeles Times, July 10, 1998.  I wrote a rebuttal to the editor, but it was not published. Professor Chung is a leading scientist in "smart materials" (materials that act similar to electronic circuits) in the United States and she is widely published.
  I thought you would get a kick out of that.  She uses crossed filaments of carbon to get a weird sort of energy out effect.  here's a video of it.  both your works have given me great ideas. "Energy from the Vacuum" - Doc. Series - Trailer - Pt. 3

author: @sk8pkl
text: hmmm you cannoth take resistance measures while things are powered... you can brake your meter doing so. when you measure resistance, your meter is acctuly putting voltage in and measuring voltage out... if you put voltage in with something else when measuring the resistance, it will either brake your meter ooorrr measure something nonsense...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I got the set up from texas instruments technical note eet-110 measuring diodes. I think what you are saying would be true if you were measuring a small resistance but here we are looking at megaohms with a tiny voltage and the difference is the readings is so large that tiny errors are of particular significance to see something is happening. Also in order to test this kind of diode we need a bias voltage that is larger than the meter voltage.

replies.author: @sk8pkl
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith hmmm... welll on a lot of meters, there is a particular place where you can plug your diode for testing. I usually do it this way lol... of course you can check conductibility both ways with ohms reading... anyways.. normal diodes conducts when there is 0.7+ volts diffrence... what about your diode?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
sk8pkl
of course you do - that's because you are measuring premade diodes - i don't know what the forward voltage drop is but it'll be high

author: @intergalactictrashcompacto6890
text: These videos never fail to amaze!  Ive been trying to print graphene for about a year now and have built a CNC laser scriber. I use an IR laser reduce graphene oxide to graphene (similar to the lightscribe/DVD mechanism) and then use a UV laser to ablate the non reduded GO along the perimeter of the circuit. I am still trying to find the optimal pulse-time, temp, and laser power before I share my results. Thanks for sharing!  Sounds like I'm going to be adding magnets to the equation now!

author: @hanneskolehmainen4414
text: Digital multimeters calculate resistance from current and voltage. Connecting external voltage source to meter will give false readings. Measure current through or voltage across your diode then reverse polarity of battery to see if it measures differently.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - will do
